title: Barry Jenkins on 'If Beale Street Could Talk' and Life After 'Moonlight' | Interview (Ep. 107)
format: Sean Fennessey talks with Barry Jenkins about adapting James Baldwin's 'If Beale Street Could Talk,' the fallout from 'Moonlight' winning Best Picture, and the 10-hour 'Underground Railroad' shoot ahead of him.

segments.heading: Barry Jenkins on 'If Beale Street Could Talk'

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: The new film. Jenkins adapted the Baldwin novel on spec around 2013 and spent four years securing the rights; a New York of interiors, shot on the Alexa 65, with the ending shifted more hopeful than the book's.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: The Best Picture winner. Jenkins made its riskiest choices out of fear; Beale Street was the same instincts made with confidence.

films.title: Medicine for Melancholy
films.note: His $12,000 debut, at its 10-year anniversary. The only film Baldwin's estate had seen when it granted him the rights.

films.title: Claudine
films.note: One of two period reference films for capturing early-'70s Harlem.

films.title: Uptight
films.note: The other period New York reference.

films.title: Selma
films.note: Where Jenkins had seen Stephan James as John Lewis, which made him hesitate to cast him as Fonny.

films.title: Race
films.note: James as Jesse Owens, the other 'too refined' role Jenkins had to put him through the ringer to get past.

films.title: Y Tu Mamá También
films.note: The first film Jenkins saw at a festival, in film school; why he wanted Diego Luna, who said yes to a 45-second part.

films.title: Straight Outta Compton
films.note: Casting director Cindy Tolan discovered Jason Mitchell (Eazy-E) here, cited as proof of her instincts.

films.title: Malcolm X
films.note: Jenkins joking about James's trajectory: 'Spike made the Malcolm X, and it is the Malcolm X.'

films.title: Border
films.note: One of two foreign films Jenkins caught back-to-back at the Arclight on midterm election night.

films.title: Burning
films.note: The other; the sequence scored to Miles Davis's 'Elevator to the Gallows' is 'the kind of thing that makes you jealous.'

films.title: Elevator to the Gallows
films.note: The Louis Malle film whose Miles Davis score is reused in that Burning sequence.

Ads: NHTSA "Drive Sober or Get Pulled Over"
